54	# Blueprint Writer
55	
56	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
57	
58	## Your Job
59	
60	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
61	
62	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
63	
64	## Directive Format
65	
66	```markdown

71

73	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex

77	
78	## Required content
79	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
80	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
81	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
82	- ...
83	
84	## Out of scope
85	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
86	
87	## References
88	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
89	- ...
90	
91	## Expected outcome
92	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
93	```
94	
95	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
96	
97	## Chapter format
98	
99	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
100	
101	```latex
102	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
105	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
106	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
107	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
108	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
109	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
110	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
111	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
112	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
113	\end{theorem}
114	
115	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
116	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
117	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
118	\begin{proof}
119	  \uses{thm:another_result}
120	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
121	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
122	\end{proof}
123	```
124	
125	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
126	
127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
139	
140	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
141	
142	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
143	
144	**The hard rule, explicit:**
145	
146	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.

148	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".

151	
152	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
153	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
154	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
155	
156	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.

1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
4	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
5	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
6	
7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
32	
33	\section{The canonical base-change map}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
37	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
39	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
40	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
41	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
42	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
43	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
44	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
45	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
46	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
47	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
48	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
49	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
50	  change, I.}
51	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
52	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
53	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
54	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
55	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
56	  \[
57	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
58	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
59	  \]
60	  It is adjoint to the morphism
61	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
62	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
63	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
64	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
65	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
66	\end{definition}
67	
68	\section{The affine case}
69	
70	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for \texttt{Scheme.Modules} morphisms}
71	
72	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
73	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
74	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
75	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
76	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
77	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
78	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
79	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
80	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
81	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
82	
83	\begin{lemma}\leanok
84	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
85	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
86	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
87	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
88	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
89	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
90	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
91	\end{lemma}
92	
93	\begin{proof}
94	
95	
96	\leanok
97	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
98	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
99	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
100	  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
101	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
102	  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
103	  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
104	  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
105	  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
106	  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
107	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
108	  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
109	\end{proof}
110	
111	\begin{lemma}\leanok
112	[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
113	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
114	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis}
115	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
116	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
117	  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
118	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
119	  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
120	  isomorphism.
121	\end{lemma}
122	
123	\begin{proof}
124	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
125	  \leanok
126	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
127	  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
128	  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
129	  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
130	  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
131	  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
132	  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
133	  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
134	  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
135	  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
136	\end{proof}
137	
138	\begin{lemma}\leanok
139	[Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
140	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens}
142	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
143	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
144	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if it
145	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of \(X\).
146	\end{lemma}
147	
148	\begin{proof}
149	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
150	  \leanok
151	  The forward implication is immediate, since the section functor over a fixed open
152	  preserves isomorphisms. For the converse, the affine opens of a scheme form a
153	  basis of its topology, so the claim is the special case of
154	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} in which the basis is the family of
155	  affine opens.
156	\end{proof}

158	\subsection{The affine computation}
159	
160	The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
161	setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
162	associative. Before stating it we isolate the one ingredient not yet available as
163	a ready-made result on which the affine reduction turns: the description of the
164	pushforward of a tilde-module along a morphism of affine schemes.
165	
166	We first record three bespoke \(\Gamma\)-fragment supports that compute the global
167	sections of an affine pushforward; they carry no external citation and stand on
168	their proof sketches.
169	
170	\begin{lemma}
171	
172	\leanok
173	[Naturality of the global-sections comparison square]
174	  \label{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
175	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
176	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, and write
177	  \(\mathrm{gs}_R : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top) \cong R\) and
178	  \(\mathrm{gs}_{R'} : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top) \cong R'\) for the
179	  global-sections isomorphisms of the structure sheaves. Then the ring square
180	  \[
181	    \mathrm{gs}_R \;\circ\; (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}
182	    \;=\; \varphi \;\circ\; \mathrm{gs}_{R'}
183	  \]
184	  commutes, where \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}\) is the
185	  top-open component of the structure-sheaf comparison map underlying
186	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\).
187	\end{lemma}
188	
189	\begin{proof}
190	
191	
192	\leanok
193	  This is the naturality of the unit/counit of the
194	  \(\Gamma \dashv \operatorname{Spec}\) adjunction read off at the top open: the
195	  global-sections map of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, under the
196	  global-sections isomorphisms on each side, conjugate to \(\varphi\) itself.
197	  Transposing the naturality square of the \(\operatorname{Spec}\) unit gives
198	  exactly the asserted ring equation.
199	\end{proof}
200	
201	\begin{lemma}
202	
203	\leanok
204	[Global sections of an affine pushforward]
205	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
206	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIso}
207	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
208	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let
209	  \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module. Then there is a canonical
210	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
211	  \[
212	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\bigr)
213	    \;\cong\;
214	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma N\bigr),
215	  \]
216	  identifying the \(R\)-module of global sections of the pushforward with the
217	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\) of the \(R'\)-module of global sections
218	  of \(N\).
219	\end{lemma}
220	
221	\begin{proof}
222	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
223	  \leanok
224	  Because \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(\top) = \top\), the global sections of
225	  the pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\) are, on underlying abelian
226	  groups, the global sections of \(N\) with no transport along the open-set
227	  identification. The two sides therefore carry the same underlying abelian group;
228	  both unwind to nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
229	  global-sections maps. These towers are reconciled by applying the
230	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the ring
231	  equation of
232	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}, which shows the
233	  resulting \(R\)-action is exactly restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\).
234	\end{proof}
235	
236	\begin{lemma}
237	
238	\leanok
239	[Global sections of an affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
240	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
241	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
242	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
243	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
244	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
245	  \[
246	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \widetilde{M}\bigr)
247	    \;\cong\;
248	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M .
249	  \]
250	\end{lemma}
251	
252	\begin{proof}
253	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
254	  \leanok
255	  Specialise Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} to \(N = \widetilde{M}\) and
256	  compose with the tilde-unit isomorphism
257	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde{M}) \cong M\) of \(R'\)-modules (which restricts to an
258	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules after applying \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi\)).
259	\end{proof}
260	
261	\begin{lemma}
262	
263	\leanok
264	[Sections of an affine pushforward over an arbitrary open]
265	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
266	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
267	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
268	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, let \(N\) be
269	  an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module, and let \(U\) be an open of
270	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), with preimage
271	  \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(U)\) in \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\). Then
272	  there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
273	  \[
274	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)
275	    \;\cong\;
276	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, V)\bigr) .
277	  \]
278	  This is the open-indexed generalization of
279	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, which is the case \(U = \top\) (so that
280	  \(V = \top\) as well). The family \(\{e_U\}_U\) of these isomorphisms is moreover
281	  natural in the open argument: the structure-sheaf restriction maps on the two
282	  sides commute with the isomorphisms, so the family is a natural isomorphism of the
283	  two presheaves of \(R\)-modules in the open variable (this naturality is

290	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
291	  \leanok
292	  The construction copies that of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} verbatim,
293	  with the top open \(\top\) replaced throughout by the arbitrary open \(U\) (and
294	  its preimage \(V\)). The point that makes this replacement free is that the
295	  \(R\)-module structure on the sections is supplied \emph{uniformly} by
296	  restriction of scalars along the global-sections ring map at the top open,
297	  independently of the evaluation open; consequently both sides unwind to the same
298	  nested restriction-of-scalars towers, reconciled by applying the
299	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the
300	  \(\top\)-level ring equation of
301	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}. Naturality in the open
302	  follows because every constituent of the construction is either a structure-sheaf
303	  restriction map or conjugation by a fixed ring map, and all of these commute with

306	  \medskip
307	  \emph{Naturality of the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) in the open (remark).} The family is
308	  in fact a natural isomorphism between two presheaves of \(R\)-modules on
309	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\): the source presheaf
310	  \(U \mapsto \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)\), with the
311	  structure-sheaf restriction maps of the pushforward, and the target presheaf
312	  \(U \mapsto
313	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U)\bigr)\),
314	  with the structure-sheaf restriction maps of \(N\) transported through the preimage
315	  operation \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\) and read as \(R\)-linear maps by
316	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). Concretely, for any inclusion of opens
317	  \(U' \subseteq U\), with \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U\) and
318	  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U'\), the square with horizontal maps
319	  \(e_U, e_{U'}\) and vertical maps the respective structure-sheaf restrictions
320	  commutes. The reason is structural: each \(e_U\) is a composite whose constituents
321	  are of exactly two kinds — structure-sheaf restriction maps (of \(N\),

326	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}). Restriction maps commute
327	  with further restriction by the presheaf functoriality axiom (using

334	  inclusion \(D(a) \subseteq \top\), in the proof of
335	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
336	\end{proof}

514	  \leanok

517	  non-circular route: checking the comparison map on basic opens yields the object
518	  isomorphism, and quasi-coherence of the pushforward is then a corollary, not a
519	  prerequisite. (One must not reverse this order: the global-sections comparison
520	  \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde{M}\bigr) \cong
521	  \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) of
522	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} alone would only upgrade to an object
523	  isomorphism once the pushforward is already known to be quasi-coherent, so
524	  quasi-coherence cannot be deduced from an object isomorphism built that way.)
525	
526	  \emph{The comparison morphism.} There is a canonical morphism of
527	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
528	  \[
529	    \alpha : \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)}
530	    \longrightarrow \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M},

532	  namely the tilde-adjoint of the global-sections identification of
533	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}. We show \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
534	
535	  \medskip
536	  \emph{Reduction to basic opens.} The basic opens \(D(a)\), for \(a \in R\), form a
537	  basis of the topology of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). By the basis-local criterion
538	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} it suffices to check that \(\alpha\)
539	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over each basic open \(D(a)\).
540	
541	  \medskip
542	  \emph{The computation over \(D(a)\).} On the source, the sections of
543	  \(\widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) over \(D(a)\) are the
544	  localization \(\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)[1/a]\) as an
545	  \(R[1/a]\)-module. On the target, since \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) =
546	  D(\varphi a)\), the sections of \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*
547	  \widetilde{M}\) over \(D(a)\) are the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over
548	  \(D(\varphi a)\), i.e.\ the localization \(M[1/\varphi a]\), regarded as an
549	  \(R[1/a]\)-module by restriction of scalars along the induced map
550	  \(R[1/a] \to R'[1/\varphi a]\). These two localizations agree: the element \(a\)
551	  acts on \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) through \(\varphi a\), so localizing
552	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at the multiplicative set generated by \(a\) is
553	  the same module as localizing \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by
554	  \(\varphi a\). Both are the universal localization of
555	  \(M\) inverting \(\varphi a\), so the comparison map \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism
556	  on the sections over \(D(a)\). As this holds for every basic open, \(\alpha\) is
557	  an isomorphism.
558	
559	  \medskip
560	  \emph{The explicit formalization route over \(D(a)\).} The agreement of the two
561	  localizations just described is the entire content, but it must be realized
562	  through the global ring \(R\), because the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
563	  sections over \(D(a)\) is built from the global-sections module of
564	  \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) rather than directly from
565	  \(R[1/a]\). The reduction therefore proceeds exactly as the conditional
566	  assembly Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} prescribes: it
567	  suffices to supply, for each \(a \in R\), the hypothesis \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) that
568	  the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) exhibits
569	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at
570	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). The \(R\)-action on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) is the honest
571	  restriction-of-scalars action carried by the scalar tower \(R \to R' \to {-}\)
572	  determined by \(\varphi\); against that structure the ring-change converse
573	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applicable, so the carrier of the
574	  \(R\)-module is exactly the one to which the localization claim refers, and no
575	  recursively-defined or ad-hoc section-level scalar action intervenes.
576	  We obtain \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transporting the already-known \(R'\)-side
577	  localization (Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}), in
578	  element-free fashion, across the open-indexed section comparison of
579	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} \emph{using its naturality in the open}
580	  (the naturality remark in the proof of
581	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}). This proceeds in three
582	  movements.
583	
584	  \emph{(1) The \(D(a)\)-level linear equivalence.} This is the component at
585	  \(U = D(a)\) of the open-indexed family of
586	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the \(R\)-linear isomorphism
587	  \[

589	      \;\cong\;
590	      \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr) ,
591	  \]
592	  using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
593	  definitional. No fresh \(D(a)\)-level construction is needed: this is precisely the
594	  open-indexed isomorphism already supplied by
595	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} evaluated at the basic open \(D(a)\).
596	
597	  \emph{(2) The naturality square for \(D(a) \subseteq \top\).} The compatibility
598	  that relates \(e_{D(a)}\) to the \(\top\)-level isomorphism
599	  \(e_\top\) (the global-sections comparison of
600	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}) is precisely the open-naturality square of the
601	  family \(\{e_U\}_U\) (established in the proof of
602	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}), instantiated at the inclusion
603	  \(D(a) \subseteq \top\): the square
604	  \[
605	    \begin{array}{ccc}
606	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, \top\bigr)
607	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_\top\;}
608	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, \top)\bigr) \\[4pt]
609	      {\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}}\big\downarrow & &
610	        \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}} \\[4pt]
611	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
612	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_{D(a)}\;}
613	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr)

617	  definitional. Because the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) is a natural isomorphism, this single
618	  square is available uniformly in \(a\); there is no per-\(a\) section-level identity
619	  to re-prove by hand. It intertwines the source restriction
620	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) on the pushforward with the (restriction-of-scalars
621	  reading of the) target restriction \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}\) on
622	  \(\widetilde M\).

625	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)} : \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top) \to
626	  \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a)) =
627	  M[1/\varphi a]\) as the \(\mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)-localization of
628	  \(M = \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) over \(R'\); this is exactly
629	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}. Apply the ring-change converse
630	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} (with \(R\)-algebra \(A = R'\) and submonoid
631	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\), so that
632	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)) —
633	  its \(\operatorname{Algebra} R\, R'\) and scalar-tower hypotheses being supplied by
634	  the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\) — to read this same map as
635	  the \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization over \(R\). The commuting naturality square
636	  of movement (2) intertwines this target restriction with the source restriction
637	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)} : \Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) by way of the
638	  isomorphisms \(e_\top\) and \(e_{D(a)}\); transporting the localization property
639	  across that square — via the transport-along-an-equivalence principle for localized

641	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) over \(R\), i.e.\
642	  \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\). No section-level computation specific to the individual \(a\)

644	  natural-isomorphism square at \(D(a)\). Feeding the family
645	  \(\{\mathrm{hloc}(a)\}_{a \in R}\) to the conditional assembly
646	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} produces the unconditional
647	  object isomorphism \(\alpha\).
648	
649	  \medskip
650	  \emph{Naturality in the open drives the transport.} The comparison assembled
651	  here, \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \circ \widetilde{(-)} \cong
652	  \widetilde{(-)} \circ \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\), is natural in \emph{both}
653	  arguments — in the module \(M\) and in the open \(U\). The naturality in the

669	  converse Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applied is the honest

698	  This single isomorphism discharges both inputs that the affine reduction below
699	  requires of the pushforward of a tilde-module.
700	  \begin{enumerate}
701	    \item \emph{The global-sections comparison.} Applying the global-sections
702	      (module-of-sections) functor to the isomorphism identifies the
703	      \(R\)-module of sections of

706	      comparison used to recognise the section-level base-change map.

709	      closed under isomorphism; hence
710	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent.
711	      In particular no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' theorem is
712	      needed for the affine lane.

715	  of this single object isomorphism — the section-level module identification and

868	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
869	  \emph{First reduction (locality).} By
870	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} it suffices to prove that the
871	  base-change map of Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an
872	  isomorphism on sections over every affine open of \(S'\). Fix one such affine
873	  open. The assertion is then local on \(S\) and on \(S'\), so we may assume all
874	  schemes in sight are affine: \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
875	  \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\), \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), with \(f\)
876	  corresponding to a ring map \(R \to A\) and \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) the
877	  quasi-coherent sheaf associated to an \(A\)-module \(M\). (It is here that
878	  quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{F}\) is used: it is what licenses writing
879	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) over the affine open.) Computing the fibre product
880	  gives \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\), and the pullback
881	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to
882	  the \((R' \otimes_R A)\)-module \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\). In this affine
883	  situation the base-change map is a morphism \(\alpha\) of quasi-coherent
884	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R')\)-modules.
885	
886	  \medskip
887	  \emph{Reduction to a concrete module map via full-faithfulness of tilde.} The
888	  functor \(\widetilde{(-)} : \operatorname{ModuleCat}(R') \to
889	  (\operatorname{Spec} R').\mathrm{Modules}\) is fully faithful, with essential
890	  image precisely the quasi-coherent modules; equivalently, the counit comparison
891	  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{N})} \to \mathcal{N}\) is an isomorphism exactly when
892	  \(\mathcal{N}\) is quasi-coherent. Source and target of \(\alpha\) are
893	  quasi-coherent, so both counits are isomorphisms; by naturality of the counit, a
894	  square relates \(\alpha\) to \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\alpha)}\), and since the two
895	  vertical counit maps are isomorphisms,
896	  \[
897	    \mathrm{IsIso}\,\alpha
898	    \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
899	    \mathrm{IsIso}\bigl(\Gamma(\alpha)\bigr),
900	  \]
901	  where \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is a concrete \(R'\)-linear map of
902	  \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\). This reframing keeps the whole computation at
903	  the level of modules and morphisms of affine schemes; in particular it requires
904	  no manipulation of section-level scalar-multiplication or module instances. The
905	  ring map underlying the pushforward at the top open is the global-sections map
906	  \(f_{\top}\) of the morphism (which for \(f = \operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is
907	  conjugate to \(\varphi\) via the naturality of the \(\Gamma\)--\(\operatorname{Spec}\)
908	  unit), and the preimage of the top open under \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is
909	  again the top open, so the identification of sections needs no transport. Any
910	  residual scalar identity in \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\) is settled by the
911	  scalar-tower compatibility of restriction of scalars.
912	
913	  \medskip
914	  \emph{Identification of the two sides via the affine dictionaries.} The two
915	  quasi-coherent sheaves whose comparison \(\alpha\) is the section-level
916	  base-change map are identified over the affine chart by the two affine
917	  dictionaries. On the one hand, the \emph{pullback dictionary}
918	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} identifies the restriction of
919	  \((g')^*\mathcal{F} = (g')^*(\widetilde{M})\) to the chart with the tilde of the
920	  base-changed module: writing \(g' = \operatorname{Spec}(\theta)\) for the induced
921	  ring map \(\theta : A \to R' \otimes_R A\),
922	  \[
923	    (g')^*\widetilde{M} \;\cong\; \widetilde{\bigl((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\bigr)},
924	  \]
925	  the tilde of the \((R' \otimes_R A)\)-module \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\);
926	  pushing forward along the affine \(f' = \operatorname{Spec}(R \to R' \otimes_R A)\)
927	  and then reading global sections recovers \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) as an
928	  \(R'\)-module by restriction of scalars
929	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}). On the other hand, the
930	  \emph{pushforward dictionary} Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
931	  identifies \(f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*(\widetilde{M})\) over \(S\) with
932	  \(\widetilde{\operatorname{restr}_{R \to A} M}\), and the pullback dictionary
933	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} then identifies its pullback
934	  \(g^*(f_*\mathcal{F})\) with \(\widetilde{R' \otimes_R M}\); reading global
935	  sections gives the \(R'\)-module \(R' \otimes_R M\). Thus, under the dictionaries,
936	  the source and target of \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) are the two iterated tensor products
937	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) and \(R' \otimes_R M\), both viewed as
938	  \(R'\)-modules.
939	
940	  \medskip
941	  \emph{Identification of \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) with the cancellation isomorphism.} The
942	  base-change map \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is the global-sections incarnation of
943	  \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap} (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}),
944	  which is constructed abstractly as an \emph{adjoint mate}: it is the transpose,
945	  under the \((\text{pullback} \dashv \text{pushforward})\) adjunctions for \(g\) and
946	  \(g'\), of the morphism obtained by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
947	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The crux of the remaining Lean work is
948	  to match this abstractly-defined adjoint mate, transported through the two
949	  dictionaries above, with the \emph{concrete} canonical \(R'\)-linear comparison
950	  between the two iterated tensor products — that is, with the associativity /
951	  cancellation isomorphism
952	  \[
953	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} :
954	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M
955	  \]
956	  of \(R'\)-modules (Mathlib's
957	  \texttt{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange}). Concretely the map
958	  sends \((r' \otimes a) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\), using the
959	  \(A\)-action on \(M\). Once the identification of the abstract adjoint mate with
960	  this concrete \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is made — the sole nontrivial
961	  remaining obligation — the affine case closes immediately, because
962	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is an isomorphism with no flatness hypothesis
963	  whatsoever; flatness of \(g\) enters only in the global (non-affine) statement
964	  via the {\v C}ech argument, not here.
965	\end{proof}

998	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), the
999	  \(i = 0\) case.}
1000	  Consider the cartesian square
1001	  \[
1002	    \begin{array}{ccc}
1003	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
1004	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]

1009	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\)
1010	  is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then the base-change map of
1011	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
1012	  \[
1013	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
1014	    f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
1015	  \]

1017	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map
1018	  \[
1019	    H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
1020	    H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
1021	  \]
1022	  is an isomorphism, where \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}
1023	  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of
1024	  \(\mathcal{F}\).

1027	  makes \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent on the affine base and thus the tilde of

1030	  \(\widetilde{M}\) over each affine open. Without it the comparison map need not be
1031	  an isomorphism.
1032	\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
The vanishing of higher direct images is
Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing}.
The statement is local on $S$ and $S'$. Hence we may
assume $X = \Spec(A)$, $S = \Spec(R)$,
$S' = \Spec(R')$ and $\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}$
for some $A$-module $M$.
We use Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback}
to describe pullbacks and pushforwards of $\mathcal{F}$.
Namely, $X' = \Spec(R' \otimes_R A)$ and
$\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated
to $(R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M$.
Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
equality
$$
(R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
$$
as $R'$-modules.
\end{proof}

\noindent
In many situations it is sufficient to know about the following
special case of cohomology and base change. It follows immediately
from the stronger results in
Section \ref{section-cohomology-and-base-change-derived},
but since it is so important it deserves its own proof.

\emph{pullback} companion: the affine description of the inverse image of a
tilde-module. It is the affine case of the classical fact that the pullback of a
quasi-coherent sheaf is computed on modules by base change of scalars.

The pullback companion is built by uniqueness of left adjoints, which is driven by
the following packaging of the per-object \(\Gamma\)-fragment comparison as a
natural isomorphism of right-adjoint functors. It is bespoke project

\begin{lemma}

\leanok
[The pushforward--global-sections natural isomorphism]
  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardNatIso}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardNatIso}
  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings. Then the
  per-object global-sections comparison of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} assembles into a natural isomorphism of
  functors \((\operatorname{Spec} R').\mathrm{Modules} \to \operatorname{ModuleCat}(R)\)
  \[
    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_* \;\circ\; \Gamma_R
    \;\cong\;
    \Gamma_{R'} \;\circ\; \operatorname{restr}_\varphi ,
  \]
  whose component at an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module \(N\) is the isomorphism
  \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\bigr) \cong
  \operatorname{restr}_\varphi(\Gamma N)\) of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
  \leanok
  The components are the isomorphisms supplied by
  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}. Naturality in the module argument \(N\)
  holds on the nose: every constituent of the comparison

\subsection{The two remaining affine-close obligations}

The affine base-change lemma below reduces, via the locality criterion
Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} and the two affine dictionaries
Lemmas~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} and~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso},
to two obligations that are isolated here as named blocks. Both are extracted from
the proof of Stacks ``Affine base change'', and both are absent from the pinned
Mathlib: the first is the base-change-of-the-base-change-map compatibility under
restriction to affine opens, the second is the coherence identification of the
abstract adjoint mate with the concrete cancellation isomorphism.

\begin{lemma}
[Affine-local compatibility of the base-change map]
  \label{lem:base_change_map_affine_local}
  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
  % "local on $S$ and $S'$" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
  % L920--926).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The statement is local on $S$ and $S'$. Hence we may
  % assume $X = \Spec(A)$, $S = \Spec(R)$,
  % $S' = \Spec(R')$ and $\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}$
  % for some $A$-module $M$."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
  change'' (proof, local-on-\(S\)-and-\(S'\) step).}
  Let \(f : X \to S\) be an affine morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, with a base-change square as above. The formation of
  the base-change map \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
  (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}) is compatible with restriction
  to affine opens of \(S'\): for each affine open \(U \subseteq S'\), the
  restriction of the base-change map to sections over \(U\) agrees with the
  base-change map of the square obtained by restricting along \(U\). Consequently,
  by the affine-open locality criterion
  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, the assertion that the base-change
  map is an isomorphism over an arbitrary base-change square \((S, S', X, X')\)
  reduces to the affine--affine case \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\),
  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) for an \(A\)-module \(M\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
  Being an isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules is, by
  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, a property that may be checked on
  sections over the affine opens of \(S'\). Restricting the cartesian square along
  an affine open \(U \subseteq S'\) produces a cartesian square over
  \(U\) whose total space is the corresponding open of \(X'\); since affineness of
  \(f\) is preserved by base change, the restricted morphism is again affine and
  \(\mathcal{F}\) is again a tilde-module over the affine chart of \(X\) lying over
  \(U\) (this is where quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{F}\) is used). The
  base-change map is the adjoint mate of the unit of the
  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, and one must verify that forming this mate
  commutes with the restriction; granting that compatibility, the general statement
  reduces to the affine--affine case. The required compatibility of the abstract
  adjoint mate with open restriction is not packaged in the pinned Mathlib, and is
  itself a base-change-of-the-base-change-map naturality statement; it is recorded
  here as a named obligation.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Adjoint-mate identification with the cancellation isomorphism]
  \label{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}
  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso,
  lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
  % L927--938).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We use Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback}
  % to describe pullbacks and pushforwards of $\mathcal{F}$.
  % Namely, $X' = \Spec(R' \otimes_R A)$ and
  % $\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated
  % to $(R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M$.
  % Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
  % equality
  % $$
  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
  % $$
  % as $R'$-modules."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
  In the affine--affine case \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\),
  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\), write \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) for the
  global-sections incarnation of the base-change map
  \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap} (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}).
  Transport \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) through the two affine dictionaries that identify its
  source and target: by the pullback dictionary
  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} together with the pushforward dictionary
  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}, the source is identified with
  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) and the target with \(R' \otimes_R M\), both as
  \(R'\)-modules. Under these identifications \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) equals the concrete
  cancellation isomorphism
  \[
    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} :
    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M,
    \qquad (r' \otimes a) \otimes m \;\mapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m)
  \]
  (Mathlib's \texttt{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange}). Since
  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is an isomorphism with no flatness hypothesis,
  this identification closes the affine case.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
  The base-change map is defined abstractly as an adjoint mate: it is the transpose,
  under the \((\text{pullback} \dashv \text{pushforward})\) adjunctions for \(g\) and
  \(g'\), of the morphism obtained by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\). In the affine--affine case the four
  dictionary isomorphisms (the pullback dictionary
  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} for \((g')^*\widetilde{M}\) and for
  \(g^*(f_*\mathcal{F})\), and the pushforward dictionary
  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} for \(f_*\widetilde{M}\) and for
  \(f'_*\) of the base-changed module) identify the source and target of
  \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) with the iterated tensor products
  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) and \(R' \otimes_R M\). The genuine remaining
  content is the coherence computation that unwinds the adjoint mate of the
  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-unit through these four dictionary isos and shows that the
  resulting \(R'\)-linear map is exactly \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\),
  \((r' \otimes a) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\). This mate-versus-
  concrete-map identification has no ready counterpart in the pinned Mathlib; it is
  the precise content that a later Mathlib bridge identifying the
  pushforward base-change map with the tilde of \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\)
  would supply. Granting it, the affine case closes because
  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is an isomorphism with no flatness.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}\leanok
[Affine base change]
  \label{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
